en.extract:
The red-masked parakeet (Psittacara erythrogenys) is a medium-sized parrot from Ecuador and Peru. It is popular as a pet and are better known in aviculture as the cherry-headed conure or the red-headed conure. They are also considered the best talkers of all the conures.


Description
Red-masked parakeets average about 33 centimetres (13 in) long, of which half is the tail. They are bright green with a mostly red head on which the elongated pale eye-ring is conspicuous; the nape is green. Also, the lesser and median underwing coverts are red, and there is some red on the neck, the thighs, and the leading edge of the wings. Juveniles have green plumage, until their first red feathers appear at around the age of four months. Its call is two-syllabled, harsh and loud.


Habitat and distribution
These birds are native to southwestern Ecuador and northwestern Peru, where they primarily live in jungle and deciduous forest. They can also thrive in semiarid regions as well as in suburban regions. While they can live up to 2,500 metres (8,200 ft) above sea level, they are usually found below the 1,500 metres (4,900 ft) mark. Their wide distribution and popularity as pets contributes to their successful introduction in other areas upon release especially in the southern and western areas of the United States as exotic feral birds.


Feral populations

Escaped cage birds are considered to be introduced in Spain. They make up most of the feral population in San Francisco, California, in the United States, which is documented in the film The Wild Parrots of Telegraph Hill by Judy Irving, based on the book of the same name by Mark Bittner. They are also seen in part of Hawaii. Breeding populations of feral parakeets have been observed in California in San Francisco, San Diego County, Los Angeles, San Gabriel Valley, Sunnyvale, Orange County, Palo Alto and Long Beach. The birds have been observed feeding on the fruits and flowers and nesting in palm trees.


Breeding
Clutches average three to four eggs and incubation is over within 23 or 24 days. Nests are usually made in tree cavities and they can breed successfully in small woodlots or even isolated trees in degraded forests. Juvenile birds fledge after 50 days with green plumage.


Conservation status
It has been the tenth most common Neotropical parrot imported into the US with over 26,000 parakeets checked in from 1981 to 1985. This bird was formerly more common in its limited range, and was reclassified by the IUCN from a species of least concern to a species that is near threatened in 1994. This is due to declining populations brought by widespread local pet trade in Peru and Ecuador, which has contributed to habitat loss and fragmentation.


References


Further reading
David Sibley (2000). The Sibley Guide to Birds. Knopf. ISBN 0-679-45122-6.
Handbook of the Birds of the World Vol 4,  Josep del Hoyo editor, ISBN 84-87334-22-9

[[File:Aratinga erythrogenys-juvenile mainly green.jpg|right|thumb|Juvenile starting to get a few red feathers on its head]]
The '''red-masked parakeet''' ('''''Psittacara erythrogenys''''')<ref name=AratingaSplit>DNA-sequence data require revision of the parrot genus Aratinga (Aves: Psittacidae) J.V. Remsen, Jr., Erin E. Schirtzinger, Anna Ferraroni, Luís Fábio Silveira & Timothy F. Wright</ref> is a medium-sized [[parrot]] from [[Ecuador]] and [[Peru]]. It is popular as a pet and are better known in [[aviculture]] as the '''cherry-headed conure'''<ref name="beautyofbirds.com">{{cite web|url=http://www.beautyofbirds.com/redmaskedconure.html|title=Red-masked Conures (Aratinga erythrogenys)|date=16 September 2021 |publisher=}}</ref> or the '''red-headed conure'''.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://sdplantatlas.org/birdatlas/pdf/Exotic%20and%20hypothetical%20species.pdf |title=Data |website=sdplantatlas.org }}</ref>{{Failed verification|date=January 2026}} They are also considered the best talkers of all the [[conure]]s.<ref>{{cite book | last = Athan |first=Mattie Sue | year = 2010 | title = Guide to Companion Parrot Behavior | publisher = Barron's Educational Series | page = 179 | isbn = 978-0-7641-4213-0 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=LLDrTg17ifEC&pg=PA179 | access-date = 2013-01-24}}</ref>

==Description==
Red-masked parakeets average about {{Convert|33|cm}} long, of which half is the tail.<ref name="beautyofbirds.com"/> They are bright green with a mostly red head on which the elongated pale [[eye-ring]] is conspicuous; the nape is green. Also, the lesser and median underwing [[Covert feather|coverts]] are red, and there is some red on the neck, the thighs, and the leading edge of the wings. Juveniles have green plumage, until their first red feathers appear at around the age of four months. Its call is two-syllabled, harsh and loud.

==Habitat and distribution==
These birds are native to southwestern [[Ecuador]] and northwestern [[Peru]],<ref name="beautyofbirds.com"/> where they primarily live in jungle and deciduous forest. They can also thrive in semiarid regions as well as in suburban regions.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/speciesfactsheet.php?id=1567|title=Red-masked Parakeet (Psittacara erythrogenys) - BirdLife species factsheet|publisher=}}</ref> While they can live up to {{convert|2500|m|ft}} above sea level, they are usually found below the {{convert|1500|m|ft}} mark. Their wide distribution and popularity as pets contributes to their successful introduction in other areas upon release especially in the southern and western areas of the United States as exotic feral birds.

===Feral populations===
[[File:Parrots of telegraph hill.jpg|right|thumb|San Francisco's Financial District]]
[[File:Aratinga erythrogenys -San Francisco -feral parrots on street lamp-8b.jpg|right|thumb|[[Feral]]s, San Francisco|alt=[[Feral]]s on a street lamp in San Francisco; one has its wings open showing the red and green on the underside of a wing]]
Escaped cage birds are considered to be [[List of birds of Spain|introduced in Spain]].<ref name="beautyofbirds.com"/> They make up most of the [[feral]] population in [[San Francisco]], [[California]], in the [[United States]], which is documented in the film ''[[The Wild Parrots of Telegraph Hill]]'' by [[Judy Irving]],<ref name="beautyofbirds.com"/> based on the book of the same name by [[Mark Bittner]].<ref>[http://www.markbittner.net/parrot_pages/wildparrots.html "The Parrot Pages"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110717132047/http://www.markbittner.net/parrot_pages/wildparrots.html |date=2011-07-17 }} - Mark Bittner 2008</ref> They are also seen in part of [[Hawaii]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://hbs.bishopmuseum.org/birds/rlp-monograph/pdfs/06-PTER-TIMA/RMPA.pdf |title=Red masked parakeet |website=hbs.bishopmuseum.org }}</ref> Breeding populations of [[Feral parrot|feral parakeets]] have been observed in California in San Francisco,<ref name="beautyofbirds.com"/><ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.dfg.ca.gov/hcpb/species/nuis_exo/ferret/ferret_issues_table3.shtml |title=HCPB-California's Plants and animals |access-date=2017-11-28 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070208070136/http://www.dfg.ca.gov/hcpb/species/nuis_exo/ferret/ferret_issues_table3.shtml |archive-date=2007-02-08 }}</ref> [[San Diego County, California|San Diego County]],<ref name="beautyofbirds.com"/> [[Los Angeles]],<ref name="beautyofbirds.com"/> [[San Gabriel Valley]],<ref name="beautyofbirds.com"/> [[Sunnyvale, California|Sunnyvale]],<ref name="beautyofbirds.com"/> [[Orange County, California|Orange County]], [[Palo Alto, California|Palo Alto]] and [[Long Beach, California|Long Beach]]. The birds have been observed feeding on the fruits and flowers and nesting in palm trees.<ref name="beautyofbirds.com"/>

==Breeding==
Clutches average three to four eggs and incubation is over within 23 or 24 days. Nests are usually made in tree cavities and they can breed successfully in small woodlots or even isolated trees in degraded forests. Juvenile birds fledge after 50 days with green plumage.<ref name="beautyofbirds.com"/>

==Conservation status==
It has been the tenth most common Neotropical parrot imported into the US with over 26,000 parakeets checked in from 1981 to 1985.{{citation needed|date=May 2014}} This bird was formerly more common in its limited range, and was reclassified by the [[IUCN]] from a species of [[least concern]] to a species that is [[near threatened]] in 1994. This is due to declining populations brought by widespread local pet trade in Peru and Ecuador, which has contributed to habitat loss and fragmentation.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.ibiologia.unam.mx/links/neo/revista/Volumenes%2018-19/18-2/ON%20(18)%20223-232.pdf |title=Archived copy |website=www.ibiologia.unam.mx |access-date=17 January 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140514104251/http://www.ibiologia.unam.mx/links/neo/revista/Volumenes%2018-19/18-2/ON%20(18)%20223-232.pdf |archive-date=14 May 2014 }}</ref>
